sections.heading: National vaccination coverage improved, but many children still did not finish on schedule
sections.body: Among children aged 12 to 23 months, full vaccination with basic antigens increased from 71.8 percent in 2022 to 77.1 percent in 2025. Full vaccination according to the national schedule also rose, but by less, from 59.4 to 62.4 percent. At the same time, the share with no vaccinations was cut in half, from 11.2 to 5.7 percent. These results show improvement at both ends: more children completed vaccination, and fewer had none. Still, finishing on time under the national schedule remained harder than completing the basic antigens overall.

sections.heading: Some regions were close to full coverage, while others were far behind
sections.body: Ilocos Region had the highest 2025 coverage on both main completion measures: 95.6 percent fully vaccinated for basic antigens and 94.3 percent fully vaccinated according to the national schedule. Davao Region was also high, at 88.4 and 86.3 percent. At the other end, BARMM had 22.1 percent basic-antigen completion and 13.0 percent national-schedule completion. This means the gap between regions was much wider than the national average alone would suggest.

sections.heading: BARMM had fewer unvaccinated children, but still had the lowest coverage
sections.body: In BARMM, the share of children with no vaccinations fell from 59.9 percent in 2022 to 28.1 percent in 2025, a large improvement. Even with that decline, BARMM still had the highest no-vaccination level among regions and the lowest completion levels for both basic antigens and the national schedule. The data show real progress away from complete non-vaccination, but not enough to close the gap in completed vaccination status.

sections.heading: Some regions reached many children, but not always on schedule
sections.body: Several regions had a large gap in 2025 between basic-antigen completion and completion according to the national schedule. Western Visayas had 86.9 percent basic-antigen completion but 60.2 percent national-schedule completion. The Negros Island Region had 81.3 percent basic-antigen completion but 59.8 percent national-schedule completion. SOCCSKSARGEN was at 80.0 percent for basic antigens and 52.2 percent for the national schedule. This suggests that many children may eventually receive the core antigens, while timing and full schedule adherence remain more uneven.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had lower completion and more children with no vaccinations
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula moved in the opposite direction from the national trend on several measures. Basic-antigen completion declined from 65.8 percent in 2022 to 61.7 percent in 2025, while national-schedule completion fell from 60.1 to 42.1 percent. No vaccinations rose from 11.8 to 20.3 percent. This makes the region one of the clearest examples in the 2025 profile where lower completion and higher non-vaccination appeared together.

sections.heading: Girls were slightly more likely than boys to complete vaccination
sections.body: In 2025, female children had 78.1 percent basic-antigen completion and 64.5 percent national-schedule completion. Among male children, the figures were 76.2 and 60.7 percent. No vaccination was also slightly lower for girls, at 5.3 percent compared with 5.9 percent for boys. The gap by sex was small, but it appeared across all three status measures.

sections.heading: Children born later in the family tended to have lower vaccination completion
sections.body: First-born children had the highest 2025 vaccination completion: 81.3 percent were fully vaccinated for basic antigens, and 67.1 percent were fully vaccinated according to the national schedule. Children of birth order 6 or higher had lower completion, at 66.2 percent for basic antigens and 56.6 percent for the national schedule. Their no-vaccination level was higher, at 9.5 percent. The pattern is not perfectly step-by-step, but overall, higher birth order is linked with weaker vaccination status.

sections.heading: Vaccination card status showed the biggest difference
sections.body: In 2025, children whose vaccination card was seen had 92.6 percent basic-antigen completion and 77.4 percent national-schedule completion, with no vaccinations at 0.0 percent. Among children who never had a card, basic-antigen completion was 14.5 percent, national-schedule completion was 11.3 percent, and no vaccinations reached 51.7 percent. This does not prove that having a card causes vaccination. It does show that documented contact with the vaccination system is strongly linked with completed vaccination.

sections.heading: Urban children had somewhat better vaccination status than rural children
sections.body: Urban children had higher 2025 completion than rural children: 78.7 percent versus 75.4 percent for basic antigens, and 64.6 percent versus 60.2 percent for the national schedule. The clearer difference was in no vaccinations, at 4.3 percent in urban areas and 7.0 percent in rural areas. The gap by residence was smaller than the gaps by region, wealth, and card status, but it still points to weaker access or continuity outside urban areas.

sections.heading: Vaccination completion generally rose with mothers' education
sections.body: In 2025, children whose mothers had college education had 82.5 percent basic-antigen completion and 69.1 percent national-schedule completion. Among children whose mothers had Grades 1 to 6 education, the levels were 59.4 and 45.6 percent, with no vaccinations at 10.4 percent. Post-secondary values were higher but flagged in the display data, while the no-education group was suppressed in 2025. Among the rows that can be used, vaccination status generally improves as maternal schooling rises.

sections.heading: Vaccination status was still much better for children in wealthier households
sections.body: In 2025, children in the lowest wealth quintile had 66.1 percent basic-antigen completion and 52.4 percent national-schedule completion. Children in the highest quintile had 88.3 and 75.8 percent. No vaccinations were 10.1 percent in the lowest quintile and 1.9 percent in the highest. The lowest quintile improved from 2022 on all three measures, but the 2025 gap still shows a strong wealth gradient in vaccination status.

takeaways: National vaccination status improved from 2022 to 2025: basic-antigen completion rose to 77.1 percent, and no vaccinations fell to 5.7 percent.
National-schedule completion remained lower than basic-antigen completion, showing that timing and following the full schedule are still major gaps.
Regional inequality was large, with national-schedule completion ranging from 94.3 percent in Ilocos Region to 13.0 percent in BARMM.
Children whose vaccination card was seen were far more likely to be fully vaccinated and far less likely to have no vaccinations than children who never had a card.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
